summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can send a crafted $regex query with a catastrophic-backtracking pattern to the parse-server class find endpoint, causing MongoDB to consume excessive CPU and dramatically increase query response time compared to a normal baseline query.
expected_security_impact: Sending a crafted $regex query with pattern (a+)+$ against a collection containing matching string data should cause MongoDB to exhibit catastrophic backtracking, resulting in query response times orders of magnitude higher than a normal query, demonstrating denial of service.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built and launched parse-server with MongoDB, seeded test data, executed a baseline query that returned quickly, then executed the crafted $regex query which took significantly longer due to catastrophic backtracking in MongoDB's regex engine.
verification_method: The reproduction measures baseline query time (normal $regex pattern) and attack query time (catastrophic-backtracking pattern (a+)+$). The verification passes when the attack query time is at least 5x the baseline time, confirming that the unvalidated $regex input causes measurable denial of service in the database tier.
evidence_produced_by_run: Baseline query using a simple substring match completes in under 1 second.
Attack query using $regex pattern (a+)+$ takes multiple seconds to complete due to catastrophic backtracking in MongoDB.
The timing ratio between attack and baseline confirms the ReDoS vulnerability is exploitable without authentication.
The parse-server logs show the MongoDB query executing during the attack but not timing out because maxTimeMS is not set by default.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
